speaker: interviewer
text: And the interview is with Esther Rudomin Hautzig. Can you tell me-- let's go back to school for a minute. What were your social activities with your schoolmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, social activity sounds like a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] structured [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] concept. We didn't have social activities. We went to school. And to get to school was sometimes very difficult because it was, after all, Siberia. And the storms were constant, and we walked in single file with-- with changing the person in the lead every few minutes, so that the wind hit one person for just a few minutes. And we would have like, chains, pick each other up to-- to go to school. And when we were in school, we were cold a lot of the time. We had to sit with our gloves. It was not well heated. And just getting through a school day was-- was a major undertaking. It wasn't just learning. It was learning in an atmosphere that made it very difficult. No paper, no textbooks. We shared. Sometimes we had to share a pencil to do homework, to do-- take notes in school, between lines, around pages. By the time we came home from school, I don't believe was ever before 4:00 or 4:30 in the afternoon. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We came home, not just I, all of the students. They had chores to do at home. When we lived with one of the people, I had to milk the cow for the-- for the people we lived with. I wasn't a very good milkmaid. I had to straighten, you know, clean the house. It wasn't-- you didn't have a vacuum. You had a broom, and you swept, and you straightened up, and you just-- and started the meal and did homework. So there was very little socializing for people during the week. I used to play a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] solitaire when I had time. I had a deck of cards from somewhere. I don't know where. And I used to lay out my fortune in solitaire. But basically, after doing these chores, I had homework. And it was a lot of homework. I went to the library. There was a little library in Rubtsovsk. That's the place I loved the best. I now volunteer for the New York Public Library once a week for four years now in one capacity, and for many, many years before that just because I feel I always will owe a debt to libraries. And that little library in Siberia was like a beacon of light. It was wonderful. It was warm. It was one room, and it had these wonderful books and a woman who gave them out. And you could be on-- on line to get a-- a translation of a novel by Charles Dickens. I mean, I read Charles Dickens in Russian in Siberia. That was my social life, to go to the library, get books, bring them home, and be able to read them. But once in a while, there was a party. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] New Year's was like Christmas here. So there-- there was a party in school, where people got together. When I had a birthday, or my mother's birthday or something, people we knew came. But it wasn't socializing with my friends. Our social life was walking to school together and coming home together. And on our free days, the children had to work for the good of the country, for the good of the fatherland, on various construction jobs. We carried bricks. Whatever manual labor we could do on our day off, on Sunday, we did, and we did it in sort of little brigades. It was hard work. You never really had time to quote, unquote 'socialize.' But if you were with friends, and you did the same work together, and you struggled through the snow together, and you-- you were in school, and you had a school newspaper, which was just a piece of paper on-- on-- on the wall, and everybody contributed a little something. And that was our school paper. You don't need a lot of-- oh, I don't know, telephones and faxes and whatever to have a social life. You meet people in school, and it's your-- -- your social life, and then you have work at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did you start your day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very early. 6:00, maybe, 6:30 was late. I had to leave for school by 7:00, 7:15 to be there for 8:00. I mean, in the winter, in storms, you had to have at least an hour to get to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it sometimes dark when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was always dark, except in-- you know, in October, there was already snow on the ground. My birthday is October 18. So I can tell by my birthday, there was snow, a lot of snow. And it didn't really melt until April.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you all keep time and know what direction you were walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had-- no, we had-- people had watches. Yes, yes. Those who didn't sell them on the [NON-ENGLISH] had watches. Yes. We always had a watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a watch, but my mother had a watch from Vilna. I still have it. We never sold it. I have this watch. It doesn't work, but we have it. I don't know. I never thought about keeping time because time wasn't that important. You went to school, the school was over, you went home. And so then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't have to go somewhere else to be exactly at 8 o'clock in the theater. Once in a while, we went to the movies. And then we certainly knew what time we had to be there because everybody was saying, oh, the Deanna Durbin movie will be in this little movie house in Rubtsovsk. It was really-- It was sometimes more relaxed than it is in 'civilization.' You know, it's a different set of priorities. Time is not one of them. You do it when you can, not by a certain hour. Of course, you had to go to work on time, to school. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the war come to an end for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The war came to the-- to an end for me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's so odd-- when Roosevelt died. We knew that Roosevelt died. And my grandmother said, who's going to care-- take care of us now? Who is this Truman? And then I knew the war ended because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father was still in-- in Europe. I mean, Siberia is not in Europe. It's in Asia, where we were. I don't remember a time like D Day here. The thing I remember most now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is when Roosevelt died. Of course, we knew that the war ended with-- with Germany through the news, but then Russia, the Soviet Union, declared war on Japan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the war with Japan was over, then war was over for all of us. And we had prisoners of war in Rubtsovsk, from German prisoners of war and then Japanese prisoners of war. But my war ended when I left Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish citizens, of which we were members-- I mean, we were part of the Polish Citizens group in Rubtsovsk-- were given permission to apply for visas to return to Poland because the Soviet government expected Poland to send Russian citizens, who went west as the German army was retreating, back to Russia. So we got permission to go back to Poland, and we did. Again, on these echelons.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- it was in March, '46. And the war was over. My father never went back to Siberia when he was released from the army. He went to Lodz, Poland, which is where we went to from Siberia. And our father met-- my father met us at the railroad station there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was there contact for you to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Letters. Don't forget, it was already after the war was over. Letters were going infrequently, but they were going. So he knew that we got permission to leave. And he knew when to expect us, maybe. And I think he met more than one echelon from Russia until he met us. I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction when you found out you were leaving Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wasn't eager at all to leave. I got used to it. And of course, I wanted to see my father. I was kind of hoping he would come back, which was so insane that I am ashamed to even say it. But I've said it before. And young people understand it. They do. They understand why I was afraid to leave something I knew, no matter how bad, for something I knew nothing about. You know, it wasn't Vilna I was going back to. I was going to an unknown place. Oh, I was terrified of leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you express that to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. She thought-- I'm sure she thought I was out of my mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Such a thing doesn't come into question. I wanted to stay just a few weeks later-- longer because I wanted to enter a contest again, a declamation contest which I lost some years before. I didn't have-- because I-- I didn't have shoes to wear to this contest. It's ludicrous. But kids understand it. And I understand it. I don't like new situations now. I've lived in two apartments in 45 years of-- almost 44 and a half years of marriage. I don't like-- I don't like to go anywhere. I like to stay where I am because I know where I am, and I know what to expect. And that is typical of a lot of Holocaust or war, Second World War-- who were children, who were shunted from one place to another. We don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you finally leave Siberia? Did you-- did you have to buy train tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We were put on these echelons, as I said before. We got permission to leave, a-- a paper giving us permission to leave. We were told when a train would come to Rubtsovsk to take us to Poland. And we boarded those trains. And they were very much like the trains in which we came from Vilna, too. They were, you know, cattle cars. The same as-- but the mood was different, you know, for-- for the grown-ups, for the adults. I'm sure my mother was very lonely for my father. And we hoped against hope that we will find people, but we knew already a year before that there was nobody there. Everybody-- almost everybody died. Two cousins of my mother's, and their mother, who was my mother's father's sister survived. And they are the ones who told us everything that happened in the Vilna ghetto because they survived the ghetto. They told me the story about Margola, which I hope we can talk about later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know a year earlier what had gone on? From the radio announcements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radio, right. But I couldn't believe that people-- that the Germans would be just killing children and-- and for no reason. I just didn't-- it just wouldn't register. I thought, this is propaganda, maybe? You know, how is that possible? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it wasn't just possible. It happened. It was a reality, not an impossibility. When we got back to Poland, I hated my year in Poland after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your way back to Poland, it was probably a number of weeks that you were on these echelons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Also about four or five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. We got-- but we packed some food with us, and we were able to get some food along the way. But we still got a ration of soup. I mean, it was hardly luxury, but it wasn't starvation. You know, we had some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get some breaks to get off the train and walk around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. The-- the train-- the breaks on the trains were usually unannounced on sidings. You know, a siding. And you wait for another train to pass by. I almost got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lost forever by leaving the train to visit a friend in another car, and the train started moving, and we were in the middle of the Ural Mountains, U-R-A-L Mountains. And luckily, someone named Nachum Reinhartz-- N-A-C-H-U-M-- Reinhartz-- R-E-I-N-H-A-R-T-Z, who now lives in Toronto with his wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] jumped out of the car, picked me up, threw me into the car, and then couldn't make it himself. And we thought he was lost. But he hung on to the caboose. You know, there's a little ladder at the end of a train, and he hung onto this caboose until the train stopped again. Oh, my goodness.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much later was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three or four hours. He was-- his hands, I'll never forget his hands. They were like no gloves holding onto the steel. Oh my goodness. Where-- I just saw him in January in Florida, and I always call him my savior. And he-- eh, [INAUDIBLE], don't talk like that. But he did. He saved me. And then we got to Poland, and that was another chapter in our lives. And I hated it because it wasn't Vilna. And we still lived in one room, and it was dark, and the city was bombed out, Lodz. Dreary, ugly city compared to Vilna. Treeless. It's an industrial center of Poland now, but it is not-- it wasn't Vilna. It could have been the most beautiful city. It wasn't Vilna. And there was no Musik, there was no Margola. There was no Lusik. There was no grandmother Sonja. There was no Resunia, no Salik, nobody. Of course, thank God there was Danka-- D-A-N-K-A--, and her sister, Masha, M-A-S-H-A. Her name in Vilna was Chajkin, C-H-A-J-K-I-N. They were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's two cousins. They are the only cousins who were saved, as I mentioned before, and their mother, Yocha Heiken-- Y-O-C-H-A. And they told us about the catastrophe in detail, how Margola died. Margola died. She could have been saved. She would have been in the same camp with Masha and Danka which was a work camp, a labor camp, not a-- an extermination camp. And people died there. They were shot. And they died of illness. But there were no-- no ovens and no killing grounds. People worked there. It was working in Estonia. They were shipped from Vilna to Estonia. And there was a selection. A selection is a [INAUDIBLE]-- is when the Germans [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] said right, right, left. I still don't-- I don't know the difference between the right and the-- I said right. Right, left. Right, left. And the old people were to the left, and able-bodied people went to the right. And Margola was put next to Danka and Masha on the right. But her mother went-- was sent to the left. And she walked over from the right side to the left side to be with her mother because she didn't want her mother to be alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I always thought of this as very heroic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some people said, who did she save? She didn't save herself, and she didn't save her mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote about it in a book called and it was just published in Japan. And a man who is an industrialist, a very able industrialist, told a translator of my book that Margola wasn't crazy. She wrote it to me. This-- I don't like to mention names without giving-- getting permission. This gentleman said, Margola wasn't crazy. I would have done the same thing. It wasn't that I would have wanted to die with my mother, but I would have thought that maybe I could save her. Maybe somehow, I could save her. Well, this is what Margola did. And her brother, Lusik, died also in a concentration camp, a natural death of-- of-- of starvation, not of being shot. This is called a natural death. And he was considered a genius in his university days. He was a mathematics and physics student. And I will jump ahead in the story because I'm talking about him. When I was in Vilna, I haunted the university. In July of 1993, I finally went back to Vilna, although my mother said to me, don't ever go back. You will make a ruin of your beautiful memories. But it was the 50th yahrzeit, anniversary of the destruction of the Vilna ghetto. And I went by myself for a week. And then a group of Vilna survivors came from Israel and America and Mexico. And I went to the university every single day. I walked. And I found a young man who found me, actually. He said, there's a Judaica faculty, a division of Jewish studies at the University of Vilna-- Vilnius now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, do you know-- he spoke English-- anything about genealogical societies in America? I said, why? He said, because I'm making-- I'm forming a group of Jewish genealogical society members in Vilnius, and I'd like to get in touch with some genealogical societies in America. And I said, it so happens that I do. And he gave me a letter he wrote. And I copied it, and I addressed envelope to 110 people and mailed it, et cetera. He said, what can I do for you? I said, you can go to the archives and look for my uncle's papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have a photograph of him. Maybe you'll find a photograph of Lusik Zunser, Elle Chim Zunser. And would you know, he found 140 pages, including Lusik's master's thesis on convex subharmonical functions, which I am now having translated-- now being March, 1995-- into English to be placed in the archives of the Technion in Israel. And the young man who is translating it said, this is not a master's thesis. This is what we would consider here a PhD thesis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every time he sends me 10 pages that he has translated, he calls to tell me what a pleasure it is to do it. And my mother couldn't mind my going to Vilna for finding that. It's like he has a monument, a [NON-ENGLISH]. This thesis is his gravestone. We know that he lived and that he worked. And I went to Vilna. Just-- just having found that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I feel was all right to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] disobey my very smart mother's suggestions. She didn't give orders. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I lost my memories of Vilna. You will make-- I made a ruin of my memories, but I have new ones. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, yes. And our house is there. And my father's business is there, except it's the police station now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reunite with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I said-- he-- he met us at the train. He knew we were-- we were coming, and we had corresponded. And how the-- the-- the meeting was, the physical meeting? The first thing he said to me, after I had gotten this gorgeous fufaika that I spoke about earlier, this jacket. And I actually got a pair of boots, leather boots, for which I worked like a slave, doing all kinds of jobs. And my father looked at me, and he said, oh, we'll have to buy you some new clothes, dear. [POLISH], which was 'little doll.' Oh. It's stupid to say I was crushed, but I was. So stupid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in Lodz, in this one room. And we had plenty to eat, but there was no sunlight in this room. And I went to school again in a one-- in an apartment which was in a half-bombed out house. And it was a Yiddish speaking school. It was called the IL [NON-ENGLISH] in Lodz after the war, and everything was taught in Yiddish, too. And we were a bunch of really sort of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stragglers. And after a while, we became a cohesive group. And I had a very good friend in that school called Heniek Cender. H-E-N-I-E-K. And his last name was C-E-N-D-E-R. He survived the Lodz ghetto and Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His mother survived Stutthoff. And we were very good friends. We did things together. We volunteered in a home for children who were saved by Christians and were then being repatriated to blood families all over the world. There's another program about that for sure. There must be children who were in that home who will be giving testimony. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after all of that, this boy Heniek committed suicide by shooting himself a year and a half after he was liberated. He was a-- he was a victim of the Holocaust as much as anyone, even though he survived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, why do I say that? He died. He died. He shot himself. With-- having lived through the Holocaust didn't make him a whole human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did Primo Levi shoot himself? He survived. He shot himself maybe 40 years or 35 years after he was liberated from Auschwitz. Who knows? He died over something that he couldn't express.

speaker: interviewee
text: His parents didn't know. It was a shock to everybody. One day, Heniek went on the rooftop with a gun he got from somebody and shot himself. Something snapped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- there were quite a few people who survived the war and later committed suicide, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they are victims of the Holocaust. Of course, there are people who commit suicide without going through the terrors of World War II. But I consider Heniek [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a victim of Nazis as much as if he would be shot at Theresienstadt or in Stutthoff. He was a very angry person who controlled his anger and his fury at what the world dealt him until he couldn't do it anymore. You know, boom. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you were in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you about the school I went to and the friends I made. And nine months later, we were given permission to have-- to get on our way to America, where my mother's brother lived, Benjamin C. Hilson. His name, of course, was Zunser, but he changed it to Hilson because my mother's uncle, when he came here in the early part of the 20th century, became a Hilson. And we couldn't go straight to America. We had to go to Sweden and live there for a while. And then I went to America by myself on a student visa, and then my parents came later. And then we emigrated officially and legally, and here we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: '47. I came in May, 1947, on a student visa, by myself, and lived with my aunt and uncle. And my parents followed several months later. And then there was a big, difficult time between their coming and getting illegal immigration visa. This was all transit. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to go to Canada to enter legally as an immigrant, not as a student. And my parents had to go to Cuba, but they didn't go to Cuba. They went to Canada also and then emigrated into the United States legally. And that's how we are Americans. My parents went through-- through Ellis Island. And my father thought-- it was-- my uncle Ben, my mother's brother, cried when they couldn't get off the boat and come straight to-- to the home that Uncle Ben and Aunt Dorothy prepared for us in Brooklyn. They had to be in-- in Ellis Island.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father said when he came, why are you crying? He was still crying days later, when they finally came out and came to our-- the apartment in Brooklyn. He said to Ben, why were you so upset? Why were you crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was clean. We got fresh towels every day. They gave us good food. Only a man like my father would find something to-- to compliment Ellis Island. I mean, after Siberia, it was not bad, and the army. So that's why we're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to school when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. The tape.
